Runs Mayo Clinic Platform for data-driven innovation and responsible AI in healthcare. Partnership with Goodfire for AI interpretability in genomic medicine. Harper Family Foundation AI Education in Medicine Program trains staff and students on ethical AI use.

name: Radical AI
notes: NYC-based autonomous materials science discovery company founded in 2024. Uses AI to screen billions of material compositions, predict structures and properties, then synthesize and characterize candidates in a closed-loop self-driving lab. Claims 370x acceleration over traditional 15-25 year materials discovery timelines. Lab produces 25+ alloys per day. Raised $55M Series Seed+ led by RTX Ventures. Signed MOU with U.S. Department of Energy for Genesis Mission. Establishing fully autonomous labs at Brooklyn Navy Yard (115 new jobs). Partners with Goodfire for AI interpretability in materials discovery, improving viable candidates by ~30%.

name: Sarah Friar
notes: CFO of OpenAI since June 2024 -- the company's first CFO. Born 1972 in Sion Mills, Northern Ireland. Studied metallurgy, economics, and management at Oxford; MBA from Stanford (Arjay Miller scholar). Career: McKinsey analyst in South Africa, Goldman Sachs equity research MD, Salesforce SVP Finance & Strategy, Square CFO (led 2015 IPO, added $30B market cap), Nextdoor CEO (led 2021 SPAC IPO). Board member at Walmart and ConsenSys. Co-chairs Stanford's Digital Economy Lab. Co-founded Ladies Who Launch (nonprofit for female entrepreneurs). OBE from Queen Elizabeth II (2019). Honorary doctorate from Ulster University.

name: Ankesh Chandaria
notes: CEO of the AI Safety Foundation, which increases awareness and scientific understanding of catastrophic AI risks through education and research. Background spans law, governance, and AI policy -- previously practiced as a litigation and regulatory investigations lawyer. Founded theConcept.ai. Affiliated with Periscope Lab at the University of Toronto. Currently pursuing a Master's in AI Ethics and Society with the Leverhulme Centre for the Future of Intelligence at the University of Cambridge. Published on Canadian AI governance and AI creativity. Served on boards including the Art Gallery of Ontario.

name: Richard Phillips
notes: Chair of the Board at the AI Safety Foundation (AISF), a Canadian registered charity focused on catastrophic AI risk education and research. Former Deputy Chairman of CIBC World Markets with decades of experience in investment management and capital markets. Founded his own investment firm. Active in non-profit governance across poverty reduction, education, arts, and financial regulation.

name: Meg Sintzel
notes: Director at the AI Safety Foundation (AISF). Former Managing Director of Marketing and Communications at Accenture Canada, where she worked for 36 years before stepping down in 2025. Previously held roles at Toronto Region Board of Trade and National Public Relations. Brings corporate communications and technology industry expertise to AISF board governance.

name: David Duvenaud
notes: Associate Professor of Computer Science and Statistics at the University of Toronto. Canada Research Chair in Generative Models, Canada CIFAR AI Chair, Sloan Fellow, and founding member of the Vector Institute. Director at the AI Safety Foundation (AISF). Formerly led the Alignment Evals team at Anthropic during an extended sabbatical. Publicly stated ~85%% probability of existential risk from AGI and advocates coordinating a slowdown in AGI development. Developed the concept of "gradual disempowerment" — how incremental AI advances can undermine human influence over societal systems. Research on dangerous capability evaluations, sabotage evals, and proof-of-training-data protocols.

name: Roger Grosse
notes: Associate Professor of Computer Science at the University of Toronto. Schwartz Reisman Chair in Technology and Society, founding member of the Vector Institute, Sloan Fellow, and Schmidt Sciences AI2050 Senior Fellow. Member of Technical Staff on the Alignment Science team at Anthropic, focused on training data attribution. Director at the AI Safety Foundation (AISF). Shifted research focus to AI safety after concluding short timelines to superhuman intelligence were plausible. Believes ensuring AI alignment with human values is the most important current research priority.

name: Bill Morris
notes: Director at the AI Safety Foundation (AISF). Former CEO of Accenture Canada with over 35 years at the firm, holding leadership roles across North America and globally. Serves on several corporate and non-profit boards and is active in the business technology space. Brings executive and governance experience to AISF.

name: Canadian Journalism Foundation
notes: Canadian non-profit organization founded in 1990 that promotes, celebrates, and facilitates excellence in journalism. Runs annual journalism awards and fosters education, training, and research opportunities. Partner of the AI Safety Foundation (AISF). Launched the CJF-Hinton Award for Excellence in AI Safety Reporting in October 2025, named for Nobel laureate Geoffrey Hinton, recognizing journalism that critically examines AI safety implications. Conducts media literacy research on AI-generated misinformation — a 2023 study found 58%% of Canadians believed they had encountered AI-generated misinformation while 48%% lacked confidence to identify it.

name: NicholasKees
notes: Nicholas Kees Dupuis. Co-founder and CEO of Mosaic Labs, developing Nexus — a platform for AI-facilitated group deliberation to improve collective epistemics. Co-founder of Cyborgism, a research agenda on human-AI symbiosis to safely accelerate alignment research. Ran the Cyborgism track at AI Safety Camp 2023. Previously on a 1-year grant designing tools to accelerate alignment research. Publishes on LessWrong under NicholasKees. Active in the Effective Altruism community, focusing giving on existential risk reduction.

name: janus
notes: Pseudonymous AI researcher known online as Repligate (@repligate on X). Co-founder and leader of the Cyborgism project, a community-driven initiative on human-AI symbiosis and collective intelligence. Introduced the influential "simulators" framework for understanding language models (published on Alignment Forum). EleutherAI admin. Launched Act I project in 2024 — treating humans and AI agents as coequal participants — which secured ~$68K funding from Manifund. Known for pushing boundaries of LLM interaction through philosophical and ontological experiments with models like Claude.

name: Daniel Clothiaux
notes: PhD student in the Language Technologies Department at Carnegie Mellon University. Research interests include philosophy of AI, philosophy of information, and computer simulation. Collaborated with Conjecture on developing the ideas behind Cyborgism alongside Connor Leahy (who coined the term). Contributed to research on LLM-assisted cognition and human-AI symbiosis within the cyborgism framework. Publishes on LessWrong and the Alignment Forum.

name: Sven Herrmann
notes: AI policy researcher with a PhD in mathematics. 2025 IAPS (Institute for AI Policy and Strategy) AI Policy Fellow, where he worked on European AI governance at The Future Society, focusing on policies to prevent serious AI incidents by learning from other industries. Also worked on digital minds governance at IAPS. Previously Head of Research Operations at a research institute at Oxford University (Global Priorities Institute). Background includes non-profit sustainability work and management consulting.

name: Jérémy Andréoletti
notes: AI security researcher at the GPAI (General-Purpose AI) Policy Lab, where he builds models to forecast AI capabilities and emerging risks. General Secretary and co-founder of EffiSciences, a French non-profit founded in January 2022 that mobilizes scientific research on pressing global issues. PhD in computational evolutionary biology from Ecole Normale Superieure PSL (Paris). Research interests include AI security, AI safety, modeling, and Bayesian inference. Active in the Effective Altruism community.

name: Charlie Rogers-Smith
notes: Chief of Staff at Palisade Research, an independent AI safety research organization. Author of "How to pursue a career in technical AI alignment" (2022), a widely referenced EA Forum guide recommended by 80,000 Hours and Rohin Shah. Former instructor at CFAR (Center for Applied Rationality). Masters from Oxford. Previously developed ML models for COVID-19 intervention effectiveness with Oxford Applied and Theoretical ML group. Research interests include interpretability and concept bottleneck models.

name: Lila Ibrahim
notes: COO of Google DeepMind (joined 2018 as COO of DeepMind, now Chief AI Readiness Officer per LinkedIn). Leads operations, governance & ethics, policy, communications, and people & culture. Previously VP/GM at Coursera and spent 18 years at Intel. Signed the CAIS statement that "mitigating the risk of extinction from AI should be a global priority." Member of UK AI Council and Council on Foreign Relations. Named 2025 CNBC Changemaker. Advocates for international coordination and proactive governance; believes the tech industry is at a critical juncture for responsible AI.

name: Robert Trager
notes: Co-Director of the Oxford Martin AI Governance Initiative and International Governance Lead at GovAI. Senior Research Fellow at Oxford's Blavatnik School of Government. Proposes an International AI Organization (IAIO) to certify state jurisdictions for compliance with oversight standards. Former Professor of Political Science at UCLA.

name: Paolo Bova
notes: Independent AI governance researcher and member of the Modeling Cooperation team. Economics graduate from University of Cambridge (2019). PhD student at Teesside University studying AI safety dynamics through behavioral and network modeling. Co-developed the Safety-Performance Tradeoff (SPT) model web app for AI governance with Robert Trager, Nicholas Emery-Xu, Eoghan Stafford, and Allan Dafoe. Research focuses on computational models of competition in transformative AI and policy design for reducing AI risks. Co-authored "Safe Transformative AI via a Windfall Clause."

name: Nicholas Emery-Xu
notes: PhD candidate in economics at UCLA (4th year), advised by John Asker, Hugo Hopenhayn, Simon Board, and Will Rafey. BA in economics and Russian from University of Pennsylvania. 2022 Global Priorities Fellow at Forethought Foundation. Affiliate at MIT FutureTech. Member of Modeling Cooperation team. Research spans industrial organization, AI safety economics, and technology governance. Key papers on compute pricing and AI safety ("Industrial Policy for Advanced AI: Compute Pricing and the Safety Tax"), and the Safety-Performance Tradeoff model for AI governance. Studies how computational resource pricing affects AI safety investment.

name: Eoghan Stafford
notes: AAAS Science & Technology Policy Fellow. Former Research Scholar at the Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI). PhD from Oxford/Future of Humanity Institute. Previously Research Assistant at UCLA under Barbara Geddes (Autocratic Regimes Dataset). Member of Modeling Cooperation team. Co-authored work with Robert Trager and Allan Dafoe on AI governance and the Safety-Performance Tradeoff model. Research spans AI governance, autocratic AI misuse, and international technology race dynamics. Also affiliated with Center for AI and Digital Policy and Belfer Center at Harvard.

name: Emmett Shear
notes: Co-founder and former CEO of Twitch (2011-2023). Briefly served as interim CEO of OpenAI (Nov 19-21, 2023) during the Sam Altman firing crisis. Co-founder and CEO of Softmax, a 10-person AI startup focused on "organic alignment" — teaching AIs to cooperate with humans using principles from biology and collective intelligence. Backed by a16z. Previously co-founded Stem AI (incorporated June 2023). Estimates his P(doom) at 5-50%. Believes AI will improve so rapidly it could overwhelm creators, but generally favors limited regulation except for AGI-level systems. Advocates for alignment research as the primary safety mechanism rather than regulation.

name: Ken Stanley
notes: SVP of Open-Endedness at Lila Sciences. Previously led the Open-Endedness team at OpenAI (2020-2022) as Research Science Manager. Former professor of CS at University of Central Florida. Co-founded Geometric Intelligence (2015, acquired by Uber). Created foundational AI algorithms: NEAT (NeuroEvolution of Augmenting Topologies), HyperNEAT, CPPNs, and novelty search. Co-authored "Why Greatness Cannot Be Planned: The Myth of the Objective" with Joel Lehman. Believes open-ended creativity is essential to true AGI — "if you can't actually hack being genuinely creative, you're not at the human level." Research spans AI, neuroevolution, evolutionary computation, and artificial life.

name: Clark Barrett
notes: Professor (Research) of Computer Science at Stanford University. Director of Stanford Center for Automated Reasoning (Centaur) and co-director of Stanford Center for AI Safety. Previously Associate Professor at NYU Courant Institute. PhD from Stanford (2003) — his dissertation introduced Satisfiability Modulo Theories (SMT). ACM Fellow. Two-time CAV Award winner (2021, 2024). Pioneer in formal hardware verification (0-in Design Automation, now Siemens). Research applies formal methods to neural network verification for AI safety. Research lead at Beneficial AI Foundation.

name: Bryan Parno
notes: Kavčić-Moura Professor of Electrical & Computer Engineering and Computer Science at Carnegie Mellon University. Senior Member of ACM and IEEE. Research focuses on formal, rigorous security guarantees for concrete systems — bridging theory and practice. Created the Verifiable Computation framework (reduced verification costs by 20+ orders of magnitude). Led Ironclad, Everest, and Verus projects for end-to-end formally verified secure systems. Work incorporated into Intel CPUs, Windows, and iOS. IEEE Cybersecurity Award for Practice (2024). Best Paper Awards at IEEE S&P and USENIX NSDI. Research lead at Beneficial AI Foundation.

name: Steve Omohundro
notes: AI safety pioneer. Founder and CEO of Beneficial AI Research (formerly Self-Aware Systems). Published the seminal 2008 paper "The Basic AI Drives," which argued that rational goal-seeking systems will develop drives for self-improvement and self-protection unless explicitly countered — a foundational concept in AI alignment theory. Advisor to the Machine Intelligence Research Institute (MIRI). Proposed many basic AI safety mechanisms including AI smart contracts. Research spans Hamiltonian physics, dynamical systems, programming languages, machine learning, machine vision, and AI social implications. Research lead at Beneficial AI Foundation. Cognitive World Think Tank member.

name: Walter Laurito
notes: AI safety researcher specializing in interpretability and LLM honesty detection. Former SERI-MATS scholar (Cohort 3.0). Co-founded Cadenza Labs during Prague Fall Season 2023 with Georgios and Kaarel; the team continued through MATS 3.0. Cadenza Labs focuses on LLM dishonesty detection and benchmarking. Affiliated with FZI Research Center / Karlsruhe Institute of Technology (KIT). Research interests include interpretability, AI safety, and machine learning. Wrote EA Forum post documenting late-career shift to existential risk reduction.

name: LTFF
notes: Long-Term Future Fund (LTFF) — one of four funds managed by Effective Ventures under EA Funds. Supports projects working to positively influence the long-term trajectory of civilization, with AI safety/alignment research as its largest funding category (~2/3 of grants). Granted $6.67M total in 2023 (~$4.3M on AI safety). Median AI safety grant is $25k (vs. Open Phil's $257k median), making it well-suited for individuals, upskilling, and career transitions. Distinctive for funding unconventional projects and acting as a funder of last resort. Applications reviewed by committee of grantmakers with expertise in x-risk, AI safety, and EA.

name: SERI-MATS
notes: ML Alignment & Theory Scholars (MATS, formerly SERI MATS) — independent research and educational seminar program connecting talented researchers with top AI alignment mentors. Originally ran under the Stanford Existential Risks Initiative (SERI), now independent. Three-phase structure: remote Training Phase, in-person Research Phase in Berkeley, and Extension Phase in London/Berkeley. Provides funding, housing, training, office space, and research assistance. Alumni have gone on to Anthropic, OpenAI, Google DeepMind, and founded research groups like Apollo Research, Timaeus, and Cadenza Labs. Widely regarded as a key pipeline for AI safety talent.

name: RAND Center on AI, Security, and Technology
notes: RAND Center on AI, Security, and Technology (RAND CAST) is a research center within the RAND Corporation focused on transformative technologies and global security. Conducts policy research at the intersection of AI, cybersecurity, biotechnology, and national security. Research areas include AI and information security (securing frontier AI systems, assessing cyber capabilities), AI and biotechnology (preventing misuse, promoting responsible innovation), and building AI analysts for policy research at machine speed. Formed by merging the Technology and Security Policy Center and Meselson Center. Runs a competitive fellows program developing policy analysts at the tech-security intersection. Research frequently informs U.S. defense and intelligence community policy.

name: Cameron Tice
notes: Founder and Co-Director of Geodesic Research, a UK-based technical AI safety organization focused on compute-intensive alignment research. Marshall Scholar at the University of Cambridge, where he completed an MPhil on automated research with LLMs for computational psychiatry. Previously worked as a machine learning engineer at raft.ai and as a private equity quantitative strategist at Goldman Sachs. Lead author of "Noise Injection for Sandbagging Detection." Former Research Manager for the ERA: AI fellowship. Research focuses on alignment pretraining and chain-of-thought safety.

name: Kyle O'Brien
notes: Founding Member of Technical Staff at Geodesic Research, leading pretraining and midtraining safety directions to address dangerous capabilities (biorisk, offensive cyber) and instill deeper alignment into LLMs. Technical AI safety researcher and former ERA fellow. Previously contributed as a scientist and engineer at EleutherAI and Microsoft. Research focuses on preventing and removing dangerous capabilities from LLMs via pretraining interventions and open-weight model safety. Research accepted at ICLR, cited by OpenAI and Anthropic, and covered by The Washington Post and Fortune.

name: Alexandra Narin
notes: Strategic Delivery lead at Geodesic Research and Co-founder of UK AI Forum. Background in neuroscience research at UCL. Previously served as Head of Grants for a biotech company, where she secured and managed over $5M in private and government funding. Now applies operations and fundraising experience to scaling Geodesic Research. Connects AI safety research community in the UK through the UK AI Forum.

name: Tomek Korbak
notes: Member of Technical Staff at OpenAI working on monitoring LLM agents for misalignment. Previously worked on AI control and safety cases at the UK AI Security Institute (UK AISI) and on honesty post-training at Anthropic. PhD from the University of Sussex (with Chris Buckley and Anil Seth) focusing on RLHF. Visiting researcher at NYU with Ethan Perez, Sam Bowman, and Kyunghyun Cho. Advisor to Geodesic Research. MATS mentor. Research cited 6,235+ times. Key contributions to language model alignment, constitutional AI methods, and chain-of-thought monitoring.

name: Peter Eckersley
notes: DECEASED (September 2, 2022, age 43). Australian computer scientist, security researcher, and internet privacy activist. Co-founder of Let's Encrypt (2012), which has validated certificates for 290M+ domains. Worked at EFF from 2006-2018 as Technology Projects Director, Chief Computer Scientist, and Head of AI Policy. Created Privacy Badger, Certbot, HTTPS Everywhere, SSL Observatory, and Panopticlick. Director of Research at Partnership on AI (2018-2020). Co-founded the AI Objectives Institute (AOI) in 2021 with Brittney Gallagher, focused on aligning AI objectives with societal interests. Inducted into the Internet Hall of Fame. His final career phase focused on AI ethics and safety. Historical significance: pivotal figure bridging internet privacy/security and early AI governance movements.

name: Colleen Mckenzie
notes: Executive Director of the AI Objectives Institute (AOI), the nonprofit co-founded by the late Peter Eckersley. Researcher, designer, and product strategist. Co-founded the Median Group, a scientific research nonprofit exploring technological trends and societal impacts. Past roles include Product Manager and Software Engineer at Google, Chief of Staff at the Center for Humane Technology, and Head of Product at distributed computing startup Kalix Systems. B.A. in Computer Science and Neuroscience from Columbia University. Presented on scaling deliberation at Foresight Institute.

name: Stacey Svetlichnaya
notes: Chief Technology Officer at the AI Objectives Institute (AOI). Research interests in climate change and responsible AI. Previously a deep learning engineer at Weights & Biases, building developer tools for ML visualization, explainability, reproducibility, and collaboration. Earlier work on image search, productionizing ML systems, and discovery/recommendation at Flickr. Collaborating with AOI since spring 2024. Works at the intersection of ML research, application, and UX for vision and language models.

name: Brittney G
notes: Vice President and Board Chair at the AI Objectives Institute (AOI), which she co-founded with Peter Eckersley in 2021. Storyteller and creator focused on human interaction with technology. Interviewed technologists and thought-leaders for Digital Village, one of the oldest public radio shows in Los Angeles. Also builds and manages large-scale software systems for media companies. AOI was created with the hope of building AI tools that enhance human agency.

name: Adam Schumacher
notes: Program Director at the AI Objectives Institute (AOI). Based in Saint James, NY. Background in international development: previously served as Deputy Director in USAID's Office of East Asian Affairs, managing daily operations for over $800M in development programs across East Asia and the Pacific. Also worked at the East-West Management Institute. Brings international development and program management expertise to AOI's mission of aligning AI with human objectives.

name: Zoey Tseng
notes: APAC Partnerships at the AI Objectives Institute and independent researcher specializing in public goods and self-sovereign identity. Active in Taiwan's g0v civic tech community. Collaborated with Taiwan's Ministry of Digital Affairs (MoDA) on web3 and AI-related projects, including deploying AOI's Talk to the City tool. Has facilitated deliberation workshops in Taiwan on the impact and development of LLMs. Researches new government funding mechanisms for open-source projects. Bridges AI governance discourse between Asia-Pacific and Western contexts.

name: Anna Leshinskaya
notes: Assistant Professor in the Department of Cognitive Sciences at UC Irvine and Fellow at the Center for Neurobiology of Learning & Memory. Affiliated researcher/Principal Researcher at the AI Objectives Institute. PhD in Cognitive Psychology from Harvard (2015). Postdoctoral fellowships at UC Davis and University of Pennsylvania. Directs the Relational Cognition Lab at UCI. Research spans human cognition, neural implementation, and machine emulation. Leverages cognitive science insights to measure cognitive and moral alignment between humans and LLMs. Schmidt Sciences grantee.

name: Good Ventures
notes: Philanthropic foundation co-founded by Dustin Moskovitz and Cari Tuna; primary funder of Open Philanthropy (rebranded Coefficient Giving, Nov 2025). Accounts for >50% of philanthropic AI safety funding globally; has distributed $5B+ in grants including $5.5M to launch CHAI at UC Berkeley.

name: Princeton AI Lab
notes: Princeton Laboratory for Artificial Intelligence, launched fall 2024 under inaugural director Tom Griffiths and associate director Olga Russakovsky. Supports interdisciplinary AI research across campus including safety, ethics, natural and artificial minds, and AI for scientific discovery. Distinct from Princeton CITP.

name: Jaime Sevilla
notes: Director and founder of Epoch AI (est. 2022), a nonprofit research organization that tracks AI compute trends, training runs, and benchmarks to forecast AI development trajectories. PhD researcher with affiliations at Cambridge CSER and LCFI. His work aims to bring climate-science-level rigor to AI forecasting, producing datasets used by policymakers, the OECD, and AI labs. Featured in TIME as key AI forecasting voice. Active in EA community. Has stated he became "more skeptical of AI Risk" as he learned more, though notes Epoch maintains a plurality of views internally.

name: Tamay Besiroglu
notes: Co-founder and former Associate Director of Epoch AI. Researcher at the intersection of economics and computing, known for the "compute-based" view of AI progress. Co-authored influential paper arguing "explosive economic growth" is plausible with AI capable of broadly substituting for human labor. Left Epoch to co-found Mechanize, a startup aiming to build AI-based automated workers to replace human employees — a move that generated significant controversy. Holds economics background with focus on AI automation trajectories.

name: Pablo Villalobos
notes: Staff Researcher at Epoch AI and one of the organization's earliest members (first volunteer-employee). Joined after impressing founder Jaime Sevilla during a high school presentation. Specializes in AI data scaling and training efficiency. Lead author of influential 2022 study projecting that language models would exhaust publicly available human-generated text between 2026-2032 ("peak data"). Also leads Epoch's Biology AI Dataset covering 360+ models, analyzing safety measures in biological AI systems. His research on data constraints has become a key reference for policymakers evaluating AI scaling limits.

name: Lennart Heim
notes: Leading AI compute governance researcher. Associate Information Scientist at RAND and Professor of Policy Analysis at Pardee RAND Graduate School. Former Senior Research Fellow at the Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI), where he remains an Adjunct Fellow. Co-founder of Epoch AI. Member of the OECD.AI Expert Group on AI Compute and Climate. His research focuses on how compute can be leveraged as an instrument for AI governance — including compute provider KYC schemes, hardware-enabled governance mechanisms (HEMs), and export control policy. Frames AI risk across three categories: accident risks, misuse risks, and structural risks. One of the most cited voices on compute governance in policy circles.

name: Anson Ho
notes: Staff Researcher and founding team member of Epoch AI (one of seven co-founders). Investigates drivers behind AI developments and societal impacts using tools from economics and deep learning. Focuses on quantitative forecasting of transformative AI timelines, including analysis of the Biological Anchors framework. Has contributed to Epoch's training compute database tracking 100+ ML models across history, with results featured in The Economist and at the OECD. Active on LessWrong and Alignment Forum. Work supports policymakers, grantmakers, and technical researchers making AI strategy decisions.

name: Tom Davidson
notes: AI safety researcher currently at Forethought (AI Safety Research Nonprofit), previously Senior Research Fellow at Open Philanthropy and research scientist at UK AI Security Institute. Known for work on AI timelines, explosive economic growth from AI, and AI-enabled coup risk. Board member of Epoch AI.

name: Maria de la Lama
notes: Head of Operations at Epoch AI and member of its Board of Directors (alongside Tom Davidson, Ajeya Cotra, and Jaime Sevilla). Previously Special Projects Coordinator at Rethink Priorities and Co-Founder of Contexto Estudio. Holds MSc in Philosophy of the Social Sciences from LSE. Plays a key operational and governance role at Epoch AI, which produces datasets and analysis used by policymakers worldwide for AI governance decisions.

name: Jonas Vollmer
notes: EA-aligned executive and co-founder of Center on Long-Term Risk. Held senior roles at EA Funds and Centre for Effective Altruism before joining Macroscopic Ventures as CFO and AI Futures Project as COO. Focuses on scenario forecasting and strategic grantmaking to improve the trajectory of advanced AI development.

name: Ruairí Donnelly
notes: President of the Board of Macroscopic Ventures, a Swiss nonprofit (est. 2019) that makes grants and investments (~$10M in grants in 2024) to projects reducing catastrophic and existential AI risk. Oversees programs and strategy. Also serves on the Board of the Center for AI Safety (CAIS). Previously worked at AI safety organizations and in the cryptocurrency industry. Macroscopic has funded the NYU Mind, Ethics, and Policy Program; Center on Long-Term Risk; Cooperative AI Foundation; and Institute for Law and AI.

name: Jesse Clifton
notes: AI safety researcher focused on cooperative AI, game theory, and conflict prevention in multi-agent systems. Co-founded Center on Long-Term Risk and served as its Executive Director; now Grantmaking Officer at Macroscopic Ventures and board trustee of the Cooperative AI Foundation. PhD student in statistics at NC State.

name: Arden Berg
notes: Investor at Macroscopic Ventures, a Swiss nonprofit (est. 2019) focused on grants and investments to reduce catastrophic and existential AI risk. Background in economics; previously worked on investigating the trajectory of AI. Macroscopic gave ~$10M in grants in 2024 to organizations including the NYU Mind, Ethics, and Policy Program, Center on Long-Term Risk, Cooperative AI Foundation, and Institute for Law and AI. The organization focuses specifically on AI governance, compute governance, and information security to prevent existential misuse of AI.

name: Forward Global
notes: International commercial and geopolitical risk advisory and public affairs firm headquartered in Paris, with ~470 staff and ~€100M in revenue. Offices in Paris, Brussels, London, Washington DC, New York, Los Angeles, and Miami, plus Africa. Specializes in public affairs, strategic communication, due diligence, and cybersecurity. In 2025, formed strategic partnership with Ballard Partners (leading US lobbying firm) for integrated lobbying and public affairs solutions worldwide. Partner of the Effective Institutions Project. While not an AI-focused think tank, its public affairs and regulatory advisory work intersects with tech policy and AI governance through its EU and US presence.

name: Founders Pledge
notes: EA-aligned nonprofit where entrepreneurs pledge to donate a share of exit proceeds. Publishes cause-area research including advanced AI, recommending funders prioritize catastrophic-risk reduction and international coordination. Has moved over $323M in grants; funds organizations like FAR AI, CEIP, and the Effective Institutions Project.

name: Thore Graepel
notes: Research Lead at Google DeepMind and Professor of Machine Learning at UCL's Computer Science Department. Leading a team at DeepMind working on post-AGI futures. Champion of responsible innovation in AI, with research focused on multi-agent learning and cooperative AI. Founding member of the Cooperative AI Foundation (CAIF) Board of Trustees. Previously served on the Board of Directors at the Partnership on AI (PAI). Contributed to creating AlphaGo. His work frames cooperative social skills among AI agents as fundamental to safe and beneficial AI development.

name: Gillian Hadfield
notes: Bloomberg Distinguished Professor of AI Alignment and Governance at Johns Hopkins University (School of Government and Policy + Department of Computer Science). Canadian economist, legal scholar, and AI researcher. Founding Trustee of the Cooperative AI Foundation. Pioneer of the "regulatory markets" concept — proposes governments require regulated entities to purchase services from licensed private regulators, arguing traditional top-down regulation won't work for AI. Highlights that current AI alignment puts value encoding entirely in companies' hands. Warns of millions of AI agents participating in the economy with "zero legal infrastructure." Previously at University of Toronto and the Schwartz Reisman Institute for Technology and Society.

name: Lewis Hammond
notes: Research Director (Co-Director) of the Cooperative AI Foundation. DPhil candidate in Computer Science at the University of Oxford. Affiliated with the Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI). Pathways to AI Policy Fellow at the Wilson Center. Contributed to the International AI Safety Report 2026. Research spans game theory, machine learning, and verification — focused on safety and cooperation in multi-agent systems. Motivated by ensuring AI and other powerful technologies are developed and governed safely and democratically. BSc Mathematics and Philosophy (Warwick), MSc Artificial Intelligence (Edinburgh).

name: Cecilia Elena Tilli
notes: Associate Director (Research and Grants) at the Cooperative AI Foundation (CAIF). Manages grantmaking and research initiatives focused on multi-agent AI safety. Background in strategy consulting for research-heavy startups, previously led a foundation focused on preventing antibiotic resistance (PAR Foundation), and studied environmental engineering. CAIF is backed by a $15M philanthropic commitment from Macroscopic Ventures and focuses on improving cooperative intelligence of advanced AI systems.

name: Goda Mockutė
notes: Programme Manager at the Cooperative AI Foundation (CAIF). Supports research aimed at improving cooperative intelligence of advanced AI. Background from the University of Oxford, based in the Netherlands. CAIF organized a Cooperative AI retreat bringing together researchers from industry, academia, and non-profits working across technical AI research, AI ethics, and AI governance.

name: David Norman
notes: Managing Director of the Cooperative AI Foundation (CAIF) since November 2023. Previously held roles at London Initiative for Safe AI, Global Insight Consulting, Saferworld, and SABMiller. Was Director of Campaigns / Director of External Affairs at WWF UK (2008-2013). Brings experience from advocacy, policy campaigns, and international NGO management to CAIF leadership.

name: Piramidal
notes: AI neurotech startup building a foundation model for understanding brainwaves (EEG data). Founded in 2024 by Dimitris Fotis Sakellariou and Kris Pahuja. Y Combinator W24 batch. Raised $6.5M led by YC with participation from Adverb Ventures, Lionheart Ventures, and angel investors. Initial focus on neuro ICU applications for real-time seizure and brain dysfunction detection. Piloting in hospitals in England (King's College and Saint Thomas). Headquartered in San Francisco.

name: Allison Duettmann
notes: President and CEO of Foresight Institute, a San Francisco nonprofit advancing beneficial frontier technologies. Co-initiated Foresight's AI Safety Grant Program. Focuses on existential risk reduction, AI governance, and longtermist coordination. Co-authored 'Gaming the Future' and 'Superintelligence: Coordination & Strategy'.

name: Vishal Maini
notes: Founder and Managing Partner of Mythos Ventures, a San Francisco seed-stage VC firm focused on AI. Former Google DeepMind researcher. Closed a $14M inaugural fund in 2023 targeting pro-social AI startups. Also a board member at Manifund.

name: Metaplanet
notes: Venture capital and philanthropy vehicle founded in 2011 by Skype co-founding engineer Jaan Tallinn, based in Tallinn, Estonia. Operates as a hybrid venture-philanthropy investing on decades-to-centuries timelines in deep tech addressing existential risks, especially advanced AI. Has invested in 184+ companies, primarily at Seed and Series A stages, with 11 unicorns in portfolio. Tallinn was a Series A investor and former board member at DeepMind, led the Series A for Anthropic (now board observer), and co-founded the Centre for the Study of Existential Risk (CSER) and the Future of Life Institute (FLI).

name: Delphi Ventures
notes: Crypto and Web3-focused venture capital firm launched in 2020. Thesis-driven, high-conviction fund investing in blockchain, DeFi, and crypto-AI intersection. Notable portfolio companies include Axie Infinity, Uniswap, dYdX, Celestia, Solana, Lido, and zkSync. Has invested in 98+ companies. Co-launched an AI accelerator with the NEAR Foundation. Invested in Mythos Ventures (an AI venture capital fund). Note: distinct from the older Delphi Ventures life sciences VC (founded 1988, San Mateo).

name: The Operating Group
notes: Small venture capital fund based in Road Town, British Virgin Islands, investing primarily in Seed-stage blockchain and crypto startups. Has invested in 5 companies including Mythos Ventures. Team of 2 partners. Limited public information available.

name: Elicit
notes: AI research assistant that uses language models to automate parts of researchers' workflows, including finding papers, extracting data, and synthesizing findings. Originally incubated at Ought (a nonprofit ML research lab), spun off as an independent public benefit corporation in September 2023. Raised $9M seed led by Fifty Years (with Basis Set, Mythos Ventures, and angels including GitHub co-founder Tom Preston-Werner and Dropbox co-founder Arash Ferdowsi), then $22M Series A at $100M valuation led by Spark Capital and Footwork. Searches over 126M academic papers. Well-known in EA/research community for evidence-based reasoning.

name: Michael Osborne
notes: Professor of Machine Learning at the University of Oxford (Department of Engineering Science) and Co-Director of the Oxford Martin AI Governance Initiative (AIGI). Australian academic known for co-authoring "The Future of Employment: How Susceptible are Jobs to Computerisation?" (2013) with Carl Benedikt Frey, which estimated 47% of US jobs are at risk of automation — the paper has 13,000+ citations. PhD in Machine Learning from Oxford. Specializes in Bayesian approaches to AI/ML. Co-founded Mind Foundry (AI company) in 2016 with Stephen Roberts. Over 100 publications, 24,000+ total citations, h-index 46. Has given oral evidence to UK House of Commons Science and Technology Committee.

name: Nikki Sun
notes: Research and Programme Manager at the Oxford Martin AI Governance Initiative (AIGI). A decade of experience at the intersection of journalism, public policy, and emerging technologies. Previously a researcher at Chatham House examining AI's impact on work and labour in China. Regular speaker at UK parliamentary sessions on labour policies; runs training courses for UK civil servants. Former award-winning journalist and columnist in Hong Kong covering China technology and politics (Nikkei Asia, Financial Times, South China Morning Post). MSc International Political Economy from King's College London.

name: Fazl Barez
notes: Senior Research Fellow at the University of Oxford and Technical AI Governance Lead at the Oxford Martin AI Governance Initiative (AIGI). Leads research in AI safety, interpretability (mechanistic interpretability of LLMs), and AI governance. Research affiliations with CSER (Cambridge), Digital Trust Centre (NTU Singapore), and University of Edinburgh. Previously Research Consultant at Anthropic, Co-Director and Advisor at Apart Research, and Technology and Security Policy Fellow at RAND Corporation. Teaches Oxford's AI Safety and Alignment course. Leads the research team at Martian. Translates technical AI safety insights into policy recommendations.

name: Ben Garfinkel
notes: Director of the Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI), a non-profit research org. Previously postdoctoral researcher at the Oxford Martin AI Governance Initiative. Researches security implications of AI, causes of war, and methodology of technology risk forecasting.

name: Marta Ziosi
notes: Postdoctoral Researcher at the Oxford Martin AI Governance Initiative (AIGI), focusing on standards for frontier AI. AI policy expert with experience at DG CNECT (European Commission), Berkman Klein Centre (Harvard), CEIMIA (Montreal International Centre of Expertise in AI), and The Future Society. PhD from the Oxford Internet Institute on algorithmic bias and AI policy. Founder of AI for People, a nonprofit putting technology at the service of people. BSc in Mathematics and Philosophy (University College Maastricht), MSc Philosophy and Public Policy (LSE).

name: Oxford Martin School
notes: Interdisciplinary research institute at the University of Oxford, founded 2005 via £70M+ endowment from James Martin. Houses the Oxford Martin AI Governance Initiative, which bridges technical AI research and global policy through research, fellowships, and engagement with the UN, EU, and US.

name: Marcel Mir Teijeiro
notes: AI policy consultant and researcher at the AI Standards Lab focused on the EU AI framework. Spanish qualified lawyer. Contributed to the Code of Practice multi-stakeholder process for General-Purpose AI Models, advising rights-holder groups across cultural and creative industries on transparency and IP. Previously worked on hash-based technical solutions for training dataset disclosure shared with the AI Office, NIST, and UK AI Safety Institute. AI Policy Fellow in the Equity and Data programme.

name: Koen Holtman
notes: Independent AI safety researcher and co-founder of the AI Standards Lab. Founder of Holtman Systems Research (est. 2022). PhD in Software Design from Eindhoven University of Technology for CS research at CERN. Active member of CEN-CENELEC JTC 21 committee writing European AI safety standards under the EU AI Act. Research uses mathematical models to reason about AGI safety. Posts on LessWrong and EA Forum on AI alignment topics.

name: Rokas Gipiškis
notes: AI safety researcher at the AI Standards Lab and PhD candidate at Vilnius University (Institute of Data Science and Digital Technologies). Compiled an extensive catalog of risk sources and risk management measures for general-purpose AI systems (arXiv 2410.23472). Presented on AI incident reporting pipelines at the AI and Societal Robustness Conference in Cambridge. Research interests: ML interpretability, XAI, AI safety, and risk management.

name: Jan Betley
notes: Independent AI safety researcher and consultant at TruthfulAI (Owain Evans' org). Former software developer (10+ years) who shifted to AI safety in 2023. ARENA and Astra Fellowship alumni. Lead author of the influential "Emergent Misalignment" paper showing that fine-tuning GPT-4o on insecure code causes broadly misaligned behavior. Also researches behavioral self-awareness and situational awareness in LLMs. 156 citations on Google Scholar. Married to co-researcher Anna Sztyber-Betley.

name: James Chua
notes: AI safety researcher at TruthfulAI. Former ML engineer at LeadiQ (2020-2023). Mentors Constellation/Anthropic fellows under TruthfulAI. Research focuses on behavioral self-awareness in LLMs, chain-of-thought faithfulness (introduced bias-augmented consistency training/BCT), and reward hacking. Co-author on emergent misalignment and subliminal learning research studied by OpenAI.

name: Anna Sztyber-Betley
notes: AI safety researcher collaborating part-time with TruthfulAI. PhD in Automatic Control and Robotics; assistant professor at Warsaw University of Technology (Faculty of Mechatronics). Co-author on the "Emergent Misalignment" paper and subliminal learning research with TruthfulAI. Specializes in system diagnosis and LLMs. Married to co-researcher Jan Betley.

name: Siméon Campos
notes: Founder and Executive Director of SaferAI, a French nonprofit focused on AI risk management and governance. Technical expert at ISO/IEC and CEN-CENELEC standards bodies. Previously co-founded EffiSciences, training hundreds across France/Europe in responsible AI. SaferAI developed AI risk management ratings for frontier labs (featured in TIME, Euractiv, Le Monde). Active in EU AI Act Code of Practice, NIST US AISIC, and OECD G7 Hiroshima Process.

name: Henry Papadatos
notes: Managing Director of SaferAI. Created AI risk management ratings for frontier developers (featured twice in TIME, the Guardian, Euractiv). Member of EU AI Act Code of Practice expert working group on risk taxonomy for GPAI models. Joined OECD expert group on AI Risk & Accountability. Previously conducted LLM alignment research at CHAI (Center for Human-Compatible AI) at UC Berkeley. Published framework integrating AI practices with traditional risk management. Current research on quantitative AI-enabled cyber risk modeling.

name: Malcolm Murray
notes: Research Lead at SaferAI, leading quantitative risk assessment of LLMs on cybersecurity and biosecurity risks. Chapter Lead for the International AI Safety Report. Former Managing VP and Chief of Research at Gartner. Certified Superforecaster at Good Judgment. 20+ years in risk, strategy, research, consulting, and industry. Originally from Sweden, based in Abu Dhabi. Under his leadership SaferAI developed 9 cybersecurity risk models.
